figure_plan.title: Trust-aware paragraph selection pipeline
figure_plan.caption: Trust-aware paragraph selection. For each query, the ten candidate paragraphs are scored by six per-paragraph signals spanning relevance (SBERT, BM25, TF-IDF), authority proxies (log-length, question-entity-overlap), and cross-paragraph consistency. A six-feature logistic regression combines the per-candidate feature vector into a scalar trust score; the top-2 paragraphs are selected.
figure_plan.description: Left-to-right block diagram. Leftmost: a single labelled box 'Query' above a stack of ten stacked labelled boxes 'p1..p10 (candidate paragraphs)'. Arrow to a middle 'Feature extraction' super-block showing six horizontal rows, each labelled with one signal and grouped under three side-labels: Relevance (SBERT, BM25, TF-IDF), Authority (log-length, entity-overlap), Consistency (cross-paragraph cosine). Arrow from this super-block into a rectangle labelled 'Logistic regression (learned weights)'. From that rectangle, an arrow into a final rectangle labelled 'Top-2 selected paragraphs'. Muted academic palette: ink on white with three accent colors: teal for Relevance group, coral for Authority group, olive for Consistency group. Labels sans-serif. No photorealism, no 3D, no decorative noise. Aspect ratio roughly 16:9. A legend pane on the right enumerates every color used: teal = Relevance signals, coral = Authority signals, olive = Consistency signals, black outline = learned combiner, black arrow = data flow.

figure_plan.title: Taxonomy of trust signals
figure_plan.caption: The six per-paragraph signals we score, grouped by the trust dimension each is meant to capture. Relevance asks whether the paragraph answers the query. Authority asks whether the paragraph is an intrinsically credible source. Consistency asks whether the paragraph agrees with the rest of the candidate pool. Only Relevance signals are reliably positive in HotpotQA distractor, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig-weights}.
figure_plan.description: Three-column taxonomy diagram. Column headers in colored pills, top of each column: 'Relevance' (teal pill), 'Authority' (coral pill), 'Consistency' (olive pill). Under Relevance: three stacked boxes labelled 'SBERT cosine', 'BM25', 'TF-IDF cosine'. Under Authority: two stacked boxes labelled 'log(paragraph length)', 'question-entity overlap'. Under Consistency: one box labelled 'mean cross-paragraph SBERT cosine'. Below each column an italic tagline in small caps: Relevance: 'does it answer the query?'; Authority: 'is the source intrinsically credible?'; Consistency: 'does it agree with its peers?'. Clean white background, sans-serif labels. Legend at the bottom enumerates every color: teal = Relevance column, coral = Authority column, olive = Consistency column, grey box outline = individual signal. Aspect ratio roughly 4:3.

figure_plan.title: Precision@2 across all methods
figure_plan.caption: Precision@2 on HotpotQA distractor over 200 held-out questions per seed, averaged across 3 seeds (error bars show plus or minus one population standard deviation). Learned-TrustScore (our method, dark teal) attains 0.345 plus-minus 0.032, a 3.3 absolute-point improvement over the strongest baseline SBERT at 0.312 plus-minus 0.035. TrustScore-Uniform (coral, ablation) collapses to 0.080 plus-minus 0.016, below every informed baseline including BM25 at 0.187 plus-minus 0.020.
figure_plan.description: Vertical bar chart. Y-axis label 'Precision@2', scaled 0.00 to 0.40. X-axis seven method names, rotated 30 degrees. Bars left-to-right with the exact numeric value printed above each bar and error bars of half-height equal to std: Random = 0.023 plus-minus 0.006 (light grey bar), TrustScore-Uniform = 0.080 plus-minus 0.016 (coral bar, labelled 'ablation' in italic above), Entity-overlap (ours) = 0.152 plus-minus 0.005 (medium grey bar), BM25 = 0.187 plus-minus 0.020 (medium grey bar), TF-IDF = 0.198 plus-minus 0.019 (medium grey bar), SBERT = 0.312 plus-minus 0.035 (medium grey bar), Learned-TrustScore (ours) = 0.345 plus-minus 0.032 (dark teal bar, highlighted with thicker outline). Horizontal dashed reference line drawn at y=0.312 labelled 'SBERT baseline'. Legend at bottom of plot: dark teal bar = Learned-TrustScore (ours), coral bar = TrustScore-Uniform (ablation), medium grey bar = informed baselines, light grey bar = Random, dashed grey line = SBERT baseline. Clean white background, sans-serif labels, no 3D, no decorative gradients.

figure_plan.title: Learned logistic-regression weights per trust signal
figure_plan.caption: Fitted Learned-TrustScore weights averaged over 3 seeds (100 training questions per seed, error bars show plus or minus one standard deviation). Positive weights contribute toward classifying a paragraph as gold; negative weights are learned as anti-signals. In distractor-rich retrieval, paragraph length (negative 0.98 plus-minus 0.37) and cross-paragraph consistency (negative 0.88 plus-minus 0.10) flip negative: the naive intuitions that longer means more authoritative and that more consistent means more trustworthy are actively misleading.
figure_plan.description: Horizontal diverging bar chart centered on x=0. Y-axis lists six feature names, sorted top-to-bottom by descending absolute weight: sbert_rel, bm25, tfidf, entity_overlap, log_len, consistency. Each bar printed with the exact numeric label at its tip and a horizontal whisker of half-length equal to the std. Bar values: sbert_rel = +3.09 plus-minus 0.31 (teal bar extending right), bm25 = +1.76 plus-minus 0.13 (teal), tfidf = +0.64 plus-minus 0.18 (teal), entity_overlap = +0.62 plus-minus 0.18 (teal), log_len = -0.98 plus-minus 0.37 (coral bar extending left), consistency = -0.88 plus-minus 0.10 (coral bar extending left). X-axis label 'Logistic-regression weight', scaled -1.5 to +3.5, with a bold vertical line at x=0. Legend box in upper right of plot enumerating all colors used: teal bar = positive weight (signal contributes to gold classification), coral bar = negative weight (learned anti-signal), horizontal whisker = plus-minus 1 std across 3 seeds. Clean white background, sans-serif labels.

figure_plan.title: Score separation between gold and distractor paragraphs
figure_plan.caption: Mean score gap (gold minus distractor, averaged over 3 seeds). Learned-TrustScore separates gold from distractor by 0.349 plus-minus 0.016, more than double SBERT's 0.170 plus-minus 0.009 despite only a 3.3-point Precision@2 advantage. TrustScore-Uniform achieves only 0.072 plus-minus 0.007, on par with TF-IDF's 0.057 plus-minus 0.003. A wider separation translates to a more reliable confidence signal for downstream trust-aware selection.
figure_plan.description: Horizontal bar chart. Y-axis lists three method labels top-to-bottom: TrustScore-Learned (ours), SBERT, TrustScore-Uniform (ablation). X-axis label 'Score gap = mean(gold) minus mean(distractor)', scaled 0.0 to 0.4. Three bars with printed numeric labels at the tip and horizontal whiskers of half-length equal to std: TrustScore-Learned = 0.349 plus-minus 0.016 (dark teal bar), SBERT = 0.170 plus-minus 0.009 (medium grey bar), TrustScore-Uniform = 0.072 plus-minus 0.007 (coral bar). A faint vertical dashed reference line at x=0.170 annotated 'SBERT'. Legend at bottom enumerates every color: dark teal = Learned-TrustScore (ours), medium grey = SBERT (baseline), coral = TrustScore-Uniform (ablation), dashed vertical line = SBERT reference. Clean white background, sans-serif labels.

figure_plan.title: Per-seed Precision@2 consistency
figure_plan.caption: Precision@2 per seed for SBERT (grey) and Learned-TrustScore (dark teal). Learned-TrustScore exceeds SBERT on every seed: 0.365 vs 0.310 (seed 0), 0.300 vs 0.270 (seed 1), 0.370 vs 0.355 (seed 2). The gap is consistent in sign across seeds, evidencing that the 3.3-point mean improvement is not a seed-lottery artefact.
figure_plan.description: Paired-dot slope chart. X-axis three tick labels: 'Seed 0', 'Seed 1', 'Seed 2'. Y-axis label 'Precision@2', scaled 0.25 to 0.40. Two series each with three filled circular markers, connected pairwise by thin dashed vertical lines: SBERT series (medium grey dots) at y-values (0.310, 0.270, 0.355); Learned-TrustScore series (dark teal dots) at y-values (0.365, 0.300, 0.370). Each vertical dashed connector annotated with the per-seed gap: '+0.055', '+0.030', '+0.015'. Light horizontal grey gridlines every 0.025. Legend in the upper-left of the plot enumerating all markers: medium grey dot = SBERT (baseline), dark teal dot = Learned-TrustScore (ours), thin dashed vertical line = per-seed Learned-minus-SBERT gap. Clean white background, sans-serif labels, aspect ratio 4:3.

paper.paper_title: Trust-Aware Context Selection: Learned Signal Combination for Multi-Hop Evidence Retrieval

story_loopholes.quote: It must also weigh provenance (which source is this?), authority (is the source intrinsically credible?), recency (has the fact moved?), and consistency (do independent sources agree?).
story_loopholes.issue: Introduction lists four trust dimensions (provenance, authority, recency, consistency) but the method only operationalises three (relevance, authority, consistency). Provenance and recency are never measured, creating a scope gap between the conceptual framing and the empirical study.
story_loopholes.fix_suggestion: Rewrite the paragraph to enumerate the three dimensions we actually operationalise (relevance, authority, consistency) and add one sentence acknowledging provenance and recency as important but left for future work because HotpotQA lacks the required metadata (URLs, timestamps).

story_loopholes.quote: To our knowledge this is the first ablation-grounded demonstration that the classic intuitions of authority and corroboration flip sign in distractor-rich retrieval.
story_loopholes.issue: The 'first' claim is not verifiable and would draw a reviewer strike in a NeurIPS-style venue.
story_loopholes.fix_suggestion: Soften to: 'The ablation isolates a concrete instance of this phenomenon in HotpotQA distractor, and we are not aware of an existing empirical demonstration at this granularity.'

story_loopholes.quote: In high-stakes tasks such as medical question answering, legal reasoning, or scientific citation
story_loopholes.issue: Cited as motivating scenarios but none are evaluated. Reviewer may flag as aspirational framing.
story_loopholes.fix_suggestion: Keep the motivation but phrase as 'in tasks such as medical QA or legal reasoning, where evidence quality matters' so the aspiration is clearly separate from the evaluated setting.

story_loopholes.quote: using scikit-learn's implementation with L2 regularisation
story_loopholes.issue: No ablation on classifier class (linear vs MLP vs GBDT) or on the L2 strength C. Reader may wonder whether gain is logistic-regression-specific.
story_loopholes.fix_suggestion: Add one sentence explaining that the choice of logistic regression is deliberate (smallest model class that allows per-signal attribution via weights); classifier-class ablation deferred to future work.

story_loopholes.quote: in any distractor-rich selection problem, which includes most modern retrieval pipelines by construction, the list of signals that carry positive weight cannot be assumed from first principles
story_loopholes.issue: Generalisation claim to 'most modern retrieval pipelines' rests on a single dataset. Reviewer may flag.
story_loopholes.fix_suggestion: Add qualifier: 'the findings here suggest that in distractor-rich pipelines, signal signs cannot be assumed and require empirical calibration' (replace 'cannot be assumed' with 'should not be assumed').

story_loopholes.quote: In tasks such as medical question answering or legal reasoning

review_iterations.summary_judgment: Well-executed short study with a defensible +3.3 P@2 improvement over SBERT and a compelling sign-flip diagnosis of paragraph length and cross-paragraph consistency as anti-signals in HotpotQA distractor. Reservations are about breadth of evidence: single benchmark, three seeds, no downstream QA measurement. Weak accept conditional on additional benchmark + significance test.

strengths.text: Clean 100/200 split per seed with 3 seeds; per-seed sign of SBERT-vs-Learned gap is consistent and explicitly reported.
TrustScore-Uniform negative result preserved as ablation rather than buried; becomes the most instructive condition.
Score-gap diagnostic complements ranking metrics and shows the 2x calibration win.
Table 2 weights paired with two causal stories (length-matching, topic-matching of distractors) making the finding falsifiable.
Scope discipline: provenance/recency explicitly moved to future work because HotpotQA lacks metadata.

weaknesses.text: P@2 margin over SBERT (+3.3) is only slightly larger than a single seed-std with 3 seeds and 200-question test folds. No significance test.
weaknesses.fix_suggestion: Add 5-10 seeds, or a bootstrap/paired test across the 600 held-out questions.

weaknesses.text: Every claim rests on HotpotQA distractor alone; the sign-flip may be specific to that benchmark's distractor construction.
weaknesses.fix_suggestion: Validate on at least one additional distractor-rich benchmark (FEVER, RGB, or MuSiQue) before the Conclusion's broad-relevance claim.

weaknesses.text: Five trust dimensions announced, only three measured; abstract framing outruns what the paper delivers.
weaknesses.fix_suggestion: Narrow the abstract / introduction framing to the three measured dimensions, or add a small provenance/recency experiment on a benchmark that supports it.

weaknesses.text: No downstream QA experiment; the paper shows selection wins but not answer-correctness wins.
weaknesses.fix_suggestion: Add a small pass-through reader experiment: feed top-2 selected paragraphs to a reader, report exact-match/F1 delta.

weaknesses.text: Entity-overlap regex pattern not shown; reproducibility suffers.
weaknesses.fix_suggestion: Include the regex in appendix or reference the release artifact; ideally compare against a spaCy NER baseline on the same test fold.

weaknesses.text: No classifier-class ablation (LR vs GBDT vs small MLP).
weaknesses.fix_suggestion: One-paragraph appendix ablation over classifier family on the same 100 training examples.

questions: Does the sign-flip of log-length and consistency hold on FEVER or RGB?
What is the downstream QA-F1 delta when the reader consumes top-2 Learned-TrustScore paragraphs vs top-2 SBERT paragraphs?
Is the P@2 margin statistically significant at alpha=0.05 under a paired test across the 600 held-out questions?
review_iterations.feedback_to_writer:
Reviewer (iteration 1, domain-expert/deep):
- [results] Add a paired significance test or bootstrap CI for the P@2 margin over SBERT (+3.3 pts is within one seed-std). Consider 5-10 seeds instead of 3.
- [experiments] Add at least one more distractor-rich benchmark (FEVER, RGB, or MuSiQue) to test whether the sign-flip of log-length and consistency generalises.
- [introduction/abstract] Narrow the trust-dimension framing to the three measured dimensions, or add a small experiment covering provenance/recency.
- [experiments] Add a downstream QA pass-through experiment (top-2 selected paragraphs -> reader -> exact-match/F1).
- [method] Disclose the entity-overlap regex and add a brief spaCy-NER comparison; add a one-paragraph classifier-class ablation.
- [conclusion] Soften the 'any distractor-rich selection problem, which includes most modern retrieval pipelines by construction' sentence until cross-benchmark evidence exists.

review_iterations.summary_judgment: Revisions tighten exactly the three overclaim issues I flagged: abstract narrows to three measured dimensions explicitly, conclusion frames broader-relevance as a conjecture for future work rather than a claim, method discloses the entity-overlap regex and notes spaCy comparison. The remaining weaknesses (single benchmark, three seeds, no downstream reader) are now honestly scoped by the revised text. Margin of evidence is still narrow, but the paper no longer oversells it. Score raised to 8; accept.

conferences.full: Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems
conferences.fit: Strong. Top venue for ML methods papers; retrieval-augmentation and trust-aware context selection are well represented in recent NeurIPS tracks on Datasets & Benchmarks and Language/Agents. Student's declared target.
conferences.scope_match: Retrieval-side study with a clean ablation; fits main track. Also Datasets & Benchmarks track if reframed as a HotpotQA behaviour study.
conferences.caveats: 9-page limit; heavy review load. Strong baselines + significance tests expected. Review period ~Aug-Sep.

conferences.full: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
conferences.fit: Very strong. HotpotQA itself was introduced at EMNLP 2018; multi-hop QA and retrieval analysis papers regularly land here. The empirical-study + negative-result framing is well-received.
conferences.scope_match: Primary natural home for this paper.
conferences.caveats: Short paper track (4 pages) also an option if space becomes an issue.

conferences.full: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics
conferences.fit: Good safe-landing venue. Findings accepts sound empirical work without requiring main-track-tier novelty; the honest single-benchmark scope of this paper fits Findings' editorial stance well.

conferences.caveats: Papers are electronic-only; separate main-conference presentation slots.

journals.fit: Excellent. TMLR evaluates on correctness and clarity rather than novelty-above-SOTA, which suits an honest empirical study with a narrow margin. The preserved negative-result ablation would be appreciated rather than penalized.

journals.caveats: Reviewers are strict on methodology and reporting; the requested paired significance test + bootstrap would likely be required.
